sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old male, presents with a 3-week history of right elbow pain, exacerbated by playing tennis.
The pain is located on the outside of the elbow, is sharp and stabbing, and radiates to a level of 8/10 with movement and 3/10 at rest.
The patient reports taking Tylenol and ibuprofen with minimal relief.
He has a history of diabetes type two and mild intermittent asthma.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes type two, mild intermittent asthma, and a surgical history of gallbladder removal 10 years ago.
He has an allergy to penicillin.
Review of systems: No other symptoms were reported.
Current medications: Metformin 500mg twice daily, Flovent twice daily, and Albuterol as needed.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient has moderate lateral epicondylar tenderness of the right elbow, with pain with passive range of motion and mild pain with resisted extension of the right wrist.
No palpable joint effusion.
A grade 3/6 systolic ejection murmur was heard on the heart exam, unchanged from prior exam.
Test Results: Normal right elbow x-ray showing no acute fracture or other bony abnormality.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow, also known as tennis elbow, likely due to overuse from increased tennis activity.
Problem list: Acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow (confirmed); Diabetes type two (status: active); Mild intermittent asthma (status: suspected).
Plan:
I prescribed Ultram 50mg once daily for right elbow pain.
I advised the patient to rest the right arm, stop tennis for 4-6 weeks, and avoid repetitive stress in the right arm.
I ordered a hemoglobin A1c level, CBC, and chem-12 blood test.
I increased the patient's metformin to 1000mg twice daily and prescribed a refill of Albuterol.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in 4 weeks to reevaluate the right elbow and consider additional imaging or physical therapy if needed.
